evals.prompt: We're stuck — every idea we have is the same as our competitors'. Give us genuinely different angles and tell us how we'd know if they work.
evals.expected_output: Should write down the obvious/competitor move, then deliberately invert it (charge more, offer less, slower-but-nicer, hide the price, add friction, remove a feature) and keep the plausible inversions. Should then design cheap, reversible behavioural experiments (A/B, soft launch, fake-door) with a behavioural metric defined up front. Should warn against trusting surveys/focus groups (stated vs revealed preference).
assertions: Writes the obvious move, then inverts it on purpose
Designs cheap reversible experiments with a pre-defined behavioural metric
Warns against surveys/focus groups (revealed > stated preference)

evals.prompt: Our survey said customers love the new idea. Should we just launch it?
evals.expected_output: Should caution that stated preference is cheap talk — people don't do what they say. Should recommend exposing people to the idea and measuring behaviour (a small reversible test) before a full launch, rather than trusting the survey. Should cite revealed preference.
assertions: Cautions that survey approval is not behaviour
Recommends a small reversible behavioural test before full launch
